content.text: The regulatory obligation to disclose AI system use, capabilities, and risks is no longer optional for most organizations deploying AI in significant contexts. A patchwork of requirements spans the EU AI Act (which imposes mandatory conformity assessments, public registration, and user-facing disclosure obligations), US Securities and Exchange Commission guidance on material risk disclosures, state-level laws in Colorado, Illinois, and New York, and sector-specific requirements from financial regulators, healthcare agencies, and employment authorities. Navigating these requirements demands a disclosure inventory — a structured map of which disclosures apply, to whom they must be made, by when, and what content they must contain.

This sub-subtopic covers the major disclosure frameworks, what they require in practice, how to build a disclosure inventory, and how to produce disclosure content that satisfies regulatory requirements without creating unnecessary legal exposure. The focus is on operational implementation: what to write, where to file it, and how to maintain it as requirements evolve.

attrs.alt: AI disclosure requirements landscape showing EU AI Act, SEC, US State Laws, and sector-specific requirements with disclosure types for regulatory, investor, user-facing, and public transparency audiences

content.text: Disclosure obligations exist because regulators and legislators have concluded that the people affected by AI decisions — borrowers denied credit, job applicants screened out, patients receiving AI-assisted diagnoses — are entitled to know that AI is involved and to understand how it works at a meaningful level. The secondary function of disclosure is accountability: organizations that must publicly disclose AI use are more likely to exercise diligence in deploying it, knowing that disclosures create a public record.

Failing to make required disclosures is increasingly a first-tier enforcement risk. EU AI Act penalties for prohibited AI practices reach 7% of global annual revenue; penalties for other violations reach 3%. SEC enforcement actions for inadequate material risk disclosure have reached into the hundreds of millions of dollars. State-level enforcement under Colorado SB 205 and New York Local Law 144 carries civil penalties. Even voluntary transparency reports, if materially misleading, can attract FTC Section 5 scrutiny.
Regulatory compliance: satisfies mandatory disclosure obligations before enforcement actions create larger consequences.
Stakeholder trust: customers and employees who understand how AI is used in decisions affecting them are more likely to trust the organization.
Investor confidence: clear disclosure of AI risk management demonstrates governance maturity to institutional investors.
Reduced litigation exposure: well-crafted disclosures that accurately describe AI system limitations reduce the factual basis for disparate impact or deception claims.
Competitive positioning: in procurement decisions, robust AI transparency documentation differentiates from competitors with no disclosure posture.
Disclosure creates a public record: inaccurate disclosures are worse than no disclosures, because they create additional liability for misrepresentation.
Over-disclosure of AI system capabilities may reveal competitive information or provide a roadmap for adversarial exploitation.
Disclosure requirements are fragmented across jurisdictions and evolving rapidly — maintaining a current compliance inventory is an ongoing effort.

User-facing AI disclosures must balance legal completeness with readability — a disclosure that satisfies regulatory requirements but is incomprehensible to the target audience may still attract regulatory scrutiny.

The EU AI Act (in force from August 2024 with phased application dates) imposes disclosure obligations at multiple levels. High-risk AI systems must be registered in an EU database before deployment. General-purpose AI (GPAI) model providers must publish summaries of training data and technical documentation. Any AI system that interacts with natural persons in a way that is not obvious must disclose the AI nature of the interaction. Deep fake content must be labeled. The conformity assessment requirement for high-risk systems is itself a structured technical disclosure to a notified body.

The SEC's 2023 cybersecurity disclosure rules require public companies to disclose material cybersecurity incidents (including AI system incidents that rise to the materiality threshold) within four business days of determining materiality. Annual reports (10-K) must include a description of material cybersecurity risks, which regulators and practitioners increasingly interpret to include AI-specific risks such as model failure, bias liability, and data pipeline vulnerabilities. Proxy statements must address board-level oversight of material risks, and AI risk oversight is increasingly expected to be explicitly described.

Colorado SB 205 (effective February 2026) requires developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to disclose the types of decisions made using AI, provide a plain-language description of the factors considered, and give individuals the opportunity to appeal AI decisions. New York City Local Law 144 requires automated employment decision tools used in hiring to undergo annual bias audits and publish the results. Illinois' Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act requires employer disclosure when AI analyzes video interviews. California AB 2930 (if enacted) would require impact assessments and transparency reports for consequential AI decisions.

The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau has consistently applied the Equal Credit Opportunity Act's adverse action notice requirement to AI-based credit decisions: when an AI system denies credit, the specific principal reasons must be disclosed to the applicant in plain language. The FDA's AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) framework requires a Predetermined Change Control Plan that discloses anticipated AI model updates and their expected performance impact. The OCC's model risk management guidance (SR 11-7) requires banks to document and disclose the conceptual soundness of models used in decision-making.
Mental Model: The Disclosure Inventory as a Registry

Think of AI disclosure requirements as a registry of obligations, not a single form. Just as a driver's license, vehicle registration, and insurance are separate documents covering separate regulatory obligations for the same activity, AI disclosure obligations are separate documents for separate audiences (regulators, investors, users, public) under separate regulatory regimes. The disclosure inventory is the registry that tracks each obligation: what it requires, when it is due, who is responsible for it, and what the current status is.

"""
ai_disclosure_inventory.py
Tracks AI disclosure obligations across regulatory regimes and jurisdictions.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional, Literal

DisclosureStatus = Literal["not_started", "in_progress", "complete", "overdue"]
Audience = Literal["regulatory", "investor", "user_facing", "public"]

@dataclass
class DisclosureObligation:
    obligation_id: str
    regulation: str
    jurisdiction: str
    disclosure_type: str
    audience: Audience
    applicable_ai_systems: list[str]
    description: str
    content_requirements: list[str]
    submission_channel: str
    frequency: str             # "one-time", "annual", "per-event", "continuous"
    due_date: Optional[date]
    responsible_owner: str
    status: DisclosureStatus = "not_started"
    last_updated: Optional[date] = None
    filing_reference: Optional[str] = None
    notes: str = ""

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        if self.due_date and self.status not in ("complete",):
            return date.today() > self.due_date
        return False

def generate_disclosure_gap_report(obligations: list[DisclosureObligation]) -> dict:
    return {
        "report_date": str(date.today()),
        "total_obligations": len(obligations),
        "by_status": {
            "not_started": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.status == "not_started"),
            "in_progress": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.status == "in_progress"),
            "complete": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.status == "complete"),
            "overdue": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.is_overdue()),
        },
        "by_audience": {
            "regulatory": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.audience == "regulatory"),
            "investor": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.audience == "investor"),
            "user_facing": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.audience == "user_facing"),
            "public": sum(1 for o in obligations if o.audience == "public"),
        },
        "overdue_obligations": [
            {"id": o.obligation_id, "regulation": o.regulation, "due_date": str(o.due_date)}
            for o in obligations if o.is_overdue()
        ],
    }

# Example disclosure obligations
obligations = [
    DisclosureObligation(
        obligation_id="DISC-001",
        regulation="EU AI Act",
        jurisdiction="European Union",
        disclosure_type="High-Risk AI System Registration",
        audience="regulatory",
        applicable_ai_systems=["Credit Underwriting Agent"],
        description="Register high-risk AI system in EU AI database before market placement",
        content_requirements=[
            "Provider name and contact",
            "AI system name, version, purpose",
            "Conformity assessment reference",
            "Countries of deployment",
        ],
        submission_channel="EU AI Act online registry portal",
        frequency="one-time then update on material change",
        due_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
        responsible_owner="Chief Compliance Officer",
        status="in_progress",
    ),
    DisclosureObligation(
        obligation_id="DISC-002",
        regulation="SEC Regulation S-K",
        jurisdiction="United States (Federal)",
        disclosure_type="Material AI Risk Factors (10-K)",
        audience="investor",
        applicable_ai_systems=["Credit Underwriting Agent", "Customer Support Chatbot"],
        description="Disclose material risks related to AI system failure, bias liability, and regulatory non-compliance in annual 10-K filing",
        content_requirements=[
            "Description of AI systems material to business",
            "Risk factors: model failure, regulatory non-compliance, bias claims",
            "Board oversight of AI risk",
            "Cybersecurity incidents involving AI (if material)",
        ],
        submission_channel="SEC EDGAR 10-K filing",
        frequency="annual",
        due_date=date(2027, 3, 31),
        responsible_owner="General Counsel",
        status="not_started",
    ),
    DisclosureObligation(
        obligation_id="DISC-003",
        regulation="Colorado SB 205",
        jurisdiction="Colorado, USA",
        disclosure_type="High-Risk AI Deployer Disclosure to Consumers",
        audience="user_facing",
        applicable_ai_systems=["Credit Underwriting Agent"],
        description="Disclose to Colorado residents when a high-risk AI system makes or substantially assists consequential decisions",
        content_requirements=[
            "Plain-language description of AI involvement",
            "Principal factors considered by AI",
            "Opportunity to correct data",
            "Right to appeal",
        ],
        submission_channel="In-application disclosure + adverse action letter",
        frequency="per-decision",
        due_date=date(2026, 2, 1),   # Law effective date
        responsible_owner="Product Legal Team",
        status="complete",
        filing_reference="Legal review memo #LGL-2026-045",
    ),
]

import json
report = generate_disclosure_gap_report(obligations)
print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

# Templates for user-facing AI disclosure notices
# Adapt to your jurisdiction and AI system context.

ADVERSE_ACTION_AI_NOTICE = """
Notice of Adverse Action — Credit Decision

We recently reviewed your application and are unable to approve your request 
at this time.

Your application was evaluated using an automated decision-making system 
(a credit scoring model). The principal reasons for this decision were:

  1. {reason_1}
  2. {reason_2}
  3. {reason_3}

You have the right to:
  - Request that a human reviewer re-evaluate your application.
  - Obtain a free copy of your credit report if your decision was based 
    on information from a consumer reporting agency.
  - Dispute inaccurate information that may have affected this decision.

To request a human review or dispute information, contact us at:
  {contact_info}

This notice is provided in compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act 
(ECOA), the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), and applicable state law.
"""

AI_INTERACTION_DISCLOSURE = """
You are interacting with an AI assistant. This assistant uses artificial 
intelligence to understand your messages and generate responses.

  - AI responses may contain errors. Please verify important information.
  - This conversation may be reviewed for quality and safety purposes.
  - You may request to speak with a human agent at any time.

For information about how we use AI, visit: {privacy_policy_url}
"""

COLORADO_SB205_DISCLOSURE = """
AI Decision Disclosure (Colorado Residents)

This decision was made using or substantially assisted by an automated 
decision system. The system considered the following factors:

{factor_list}

You have the right to:
  - Request a human review of this decision within 90 days.
  - Correct inaccurate personal information that may have affected this decision.

To exercise these rights, contact: {contact_info}
"""

def generate_adverse_action_notice(reasons: list[str], contact_info: str) -> str:
    padded = (reasons + ["N/A"] * 3)[:3]
    return ADVERSE_ACTION_AI_NOTICE.format(
        reason_1=padded[0],
        reason_2=padded[1],
        reason_3=padded[2],
        contact_info=contact_info,
    )

example = generate_adverse_action_notice(
    reasons=["Insufficient credit history", "High debt-to-income ratio"],
    contact_info="appeals@lender.com or 1-800-555-0100",
)
print(example)

A lending marketplace deployed an AI underwriting model that approved or declined loan applications. Under ECOA and Regulation B, adverse action notices are mandatory when a credit application is denied, and the notices must state the specific reasons for denial. When the AI model was introduced, the legal team worked with the data science team to extract the top-three feature importance scores from each negative decision and map them to consumer-readable reason codes. The mapping required both technical work (ensuring explanations from the model were accurate and stable) and legal review (ensuring the reason codes satisfied ECOA's specificity requirements). The solution was tested against a test dataset of known denial scenarios and reviewed by outside counsel before deployment. The firm also published a public-facing AI system card describing the model's purpose, training approach, and the adverse action reason code methodology — a disclosure that proved useful when the CFPB inquired about the lending program's AI use.
Disclosing That AI Is Used Without Explaining What It Does

'We use AI in our decision-making process' satisfies the letter of some disclosure requirements but fails the spirit — and may not satisfy requirements like Colorado SB 205 that mandate specific factor disclosure. Disclosures must describe the AI's role with sufficient specificity that a reasonable person could understand how it affected the decision.
Treating Disclosure as a One-Time Event

AI disclosure requirements apply throughout the system lifecycle. When a model is retrained and performance characteristics change materially, disclosures that describe the model must be updated. When a new use case is added, the disclosure inventory must be reassessed for new obligations. Disclosure is ongoing, not a deployment-day task.
Maintain a disclosure inventory in a GRC tool that maps each regulatory disclosure obligation to the AI systems it covers, the responsible owner, and the current status.
Review the disclosure inventory when new AI systems are deployed, when models are retrained, and when new regulations are enacted.
Work with legal counsel to draft user-facing disclosures — technical accuracy is necessary but not sufficient; the disclosure must also satisfy the specific regulatory language.
Test adverse action notices against the model's actual decision logic, not against general descriptions of how the model works.
For EU AI Act obligations, engage a notified body early — conformity assessments for high-risk systems can take three to six months.
Board-level reporting on AI risks should be explicit, not implicit — name the AI systems, describe their risk profile, and describe the oversight mechanisms in place.

As the number of AI systems grows, the disclosure inventory grows with it. Automating the extraction of decision factors from AI models (for adverse action notices) and the population of regulatory filing templates from the AI system inventory reduces the per-system effort required for disclosure maintenance. Legal technology platforms are beginning to support AI disclosure obligation tracking as a module within contract lifecycle management and regulatory compliance platforms.

Disclosure documents, particularly those filed with regulators, create a public record. Inaccurate disclosures — whether by overstating AI system reliability or understating known risks — can constitute material misrepresentations. Before any disclosure is filed or published, it should be reviewed by both the AI technical team (for factual accuracy about system capabilities and limitations) and legal counsel (for regulatory compliance and liability implications). Version-control all disclosure documents so that the content and date of each version can be reconstructed.
AI disclosure requirements come from multiple overlapping regulatory regimes: EU AI Act, SEC material risk disclosure rules, US state laws, and sector-specific regulations.
The EU AI Act requires high-risk AI systems to be registered in a public EU database before deployment and mandates user-facing disclosure when AI interacts with natural persons.
SEC rules require public companies to disclose AI as a material risk factor in annual reports and to disclose material AI incidents within four business days of determining materiality.
ECOA requires adverse action notices for AI credit decisions to include specific, consumer-readable reasons — 'the model said no' is not sufficient.
A disclosure inventory maps each obligation to the AI systems it covers, the responsible owner, the content requirements, and the current compliance status.
Disclosure is ongoing — model retraining, new use cases, and new regulations all trigger disclosure inventory reviews.
User-facing disclosures must be technically accurate and satisfy regulatory specificity requirements — general boilerplate about 'using AI' rarely satisfies either test.
